claim: Quality gap between closed-source and open-source models is steadily closing; Kimi K2 (trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts) matches or exceeds GPT-4o and Claude on benchmarks
actionable: Evaluate open-source models like Kimi K2, GLM-4.6, and Qwen3-Coder on your tasks before defaulting to closed-source APIs.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Open-source models offer three critical advantages: latency control (time to first token), reliability at scale, and cost—factors that directly impact user experience
actionable: When selecting models for production, prioritize latency, reliability, and cost as first-class concerns beyond raw benchmark scores.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Kimi K2 uses interleaved thinking (reasoning interspersed with tool calls) rather than chain-of-thought, allowing iterative reflection after each action—this improves tool use on agentic tasks
actionable: For agentic coding tasks, prefer models with interleaved reasoning capabilities; they adapt dynamically based on intermediate results.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Kimi K2 was trained with a five-step synthetic tool-calling pipeline: gathered 3,000 tools from GitHub, generated 20,000+ synthetic tools via clustering, created synthetic tasks with diverse scenarios, and filtered for quality
actionable: When fine-tuning models for tool use, generate synthetic tool catalogs with diverse clustering and task scenarios to improve generalization.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: GLM-4.6 is 30% more efficient than 4.5 in token consumption—better reasoner with lower inference cost and latency
actionable: When upgrading models, prioritize those showing token-efficiency gains—they reduce both cost and latency.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Autocomplete has asymmetric patterns (long prefill/short decode) requiring KV cache reuse, KV-aware routing, and n-gram speculation for sub-200ms latency
actionable: For code completion agents, implement KV cache persistence across requests and n-gram-based draft token speculation to meet sub-200ms latency targets.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Specialized coding models (Qwen3-Coder) show decreased interest as general agentic models improve, but remain solid for prototyping or high-token repetitive tasks
actionable: Prefer general agentic models like Kimi K2 for production; use coding specialists only if you exhaust tokens on basic tasks.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale

claim: Using API proxies (e.g., LiteLLM) to secretly route Claude/GPT requests to open-source models at the same CLI allows cheaper, faster inference while maintaining interface compatibility
actionable: Use lightweight API proxies to experiment with open-source alternatives without rewriting existing integration code.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Alex Ker: How Open source Models Actually Run AI Coding at Scale
